meta.name: CES-D Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale
meta.fullName: Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale
meta.description: The CES-D is a 20-item self-report depression scale developed for use in the general population. It measures the frequency of depressive symptoms experienced during the past week and is one of the most widely used instruments in epidemiological research on depression.
meta.author: Radloff (1977)
meta.reference: Radloff LS. The CES-D Scale: A self-report depression scale for research in the general population. Appl Psychol Meas. 1977;1(3):385-401.
meta.instruction: Below is a list of ways you might have felt or behaved. Please indicate how often you have felt this way during the past week.

commonChoices.label: Rarely or none of the time (less than 1 day)
Some or a little of the time (1-2 days)
Occasionally or a moderate amount of time (3-4 days)
Most or all of the time (5-7 days)

questions.text: I was bothered by things that usually don't bother me
I did not feel like eating; my appetite was poor
I felt that I could not shake off the blues even with help from my family or friends
I felt that I was just as good as other people
I had trouble keeping my mind on what I was doing
I felt that everything I did was an effort
I felt hopeful about the future
I thought my life had been a failure
I felt that people disliked me

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your score is within the normal range, indicating no significant depressive symptoms. You appear to be coping well with everyday stressors, and your emotional functioning is healthy.
suggestions: Continue maintaining a healthy lifestyle with regular exercise and balanced nutrition
Keep up regular social activities and nurture close relationships
Practice preventive mental health habits such as adequate sleep and stress management
Occasional mood fluctuations are a normal part of life

ranges.label: Mild Depression
ranges.description: Your score suggests mild depressive symptoms that may be affecting your daily life. At this level, you may notice reduced energy, changes in sleep or appetite, or a general sense of sadness that comes and goes.
suggestions: Pay attention to your emotional well-being and track mood patterns
Maintain regular sleep, exercise, and social routines
Try talking to a trusted friend or family member about how you feel
Consider journaling or mindfulness practices to process emotions
If symptoms persist for more than two weeks, consider consulting a professional

ranges.label: Moderate Depression
ranges.description: Your score indicates moderate depressive symptoms that may be significantly affecting your daily functioning. You may experience persistent low mood, difficulty concentrating, social withdrawal, or loss of interest in previously enjoyed activities.
suggestions: Consider consulting a mental health professional for a thorough evaluation
Maintain a structured daily routine to provide stability
Engage in regular physical activity, which has strong evidence for reducing depression
Avoid isolation; stay connected with supportive people in your life
Consider evidence-based psychotherapy such as cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT)

ranges.label: Severe Depression
ranges.description: Your score suggests severe depressive symptoms that are likely significantly impairing your daily functioning, relationships, and quality of life. Professional help is strongly recommended at this level.
suggestions: Seek consultation with a mental health professional as soon as possible
If you have thoughts of self-harm or suicide, please call a crisis hotline immediately (e.g., 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline)
Do not stay alone; inform a trusted person about your situation
Professional treatment, including therapy and/or medication, can be very effective
Focus on basic self-care: sleep, nutrition, and gentle physical activity
